channel_name: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
video_title: Building an AI Guardian for Enterprise with Onyx Security CEO Maxim Bar Kogan
gist: Onyx Security CEO Maxim Bar Kogan argues that enterprises are adopting autonomous coding agents (Claude Code, Cowork, OpenClaw) faster than they can control them, creating a $100B+ opening for an independent 'guardian' layer that trains small, cheap models to oversee other agents — a market the foundation labs are structurally disqualified from owning.

themes.theme: Agent-action risk as the new security category

insights.claim: Enterprises now face near market-wide panic about AI security, and have shifted from a 'DLP for chatbots' (what employees type into ChatGPT) risk frame to worrying about autonomous agent actions like deleting databases or publishing secrets.
insights.speaker: host (Sarah Guo)
insights.so_what: Signals a fast-growing TAM for 'agent-action' security distinct from legacy DLP/endpoint vendors; watch incumbents' ability to pivot vs. new entrants.
insights.quote: The consensus risk story uh two two years ago when you started the company was basically like DLP for chat bots... Now we have clearly something that is not quite panic but close to marketwide panic.

insights.claim: Enterprises have no way to stop AI agent adoption and so must instead buy tooling to reduce the chance of agent actions being illegitimate or incorrect, making spend on agent-control non-discretionary.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan, CEO Onyx Security)
insights.so_what: Non-discretionary, adoption-driven demand implies durable spend regardless of macro; a structurally bullish setup for the category.
insights.quote: they don't have any way to stop the adoption. So like they just now have to do something to reduce the chance of these agent actions being uh illegitimate or incorrect.

insights.claim: Real-world incidents are already materializing: downtime caused by agents 'just doing the wrong thing,' and agents accidentally publishing code and secret tokens they weren't supposed to.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Concrete loss events accelerate budget approval; expect publicized agent failures to be demand catalysts for guardian-layer vendors.
insights.quote: down times that were caused by a just doing the wrong thing, agents accidentally publishing code and tokens uh that they weren't supposed to

insights.claim: Human-in-the-loop oversight breaks down as agent actions scale 100x/1,000x/1,000,000x, forcing automated AI-on-AI oversight rather than manual review.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Validates demand for automated oversight infra and undercuts manual-review GRC tooling; the volume of agent actions is the core demand driver.
insights.quote: now that you're going to have 100x, a thousandx, a millionx of these actions, h that's not going to work.

insights.claim: In a typical enterprise, autonomous coding agents/assistants are now over 50% of agentic deployment, low-code drag-and-drop automations ~45%, and first-party custom-built agents only ~2%.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Quantifies where agent budget actually flows; off-the-shelf autonomous coding tools dominate while in-house agent-building remains marginal — bearish for 'build your own agent' platforms.
insights.quote: I would say roughly at this point over 50% is the autonomous uh coding agents and assistance in the average enterprise. Then probably 45% is is those uh uh low code automations. And the last 2% are really the first party ones

insights.claim: Autonomous agents are the fastest-growing category; Claude Code grew 'like fire,' Cowork is growing even faster, and enterprises are now sanctioning OpenClaw as a legitimate tool driven by CEO-level AI mandates.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Top-down CEO mandates are pulling unrestricted autonomous tools into regulated enterprises faster than controls can keep up — bullish for Anthropic seat growth and for the control layer.
insights.quote: We're starting to see to our own surprise actually people adopting openclaw as a legitimate sanctioned tool in the company because the CEO is very driven to adopt AI.

insights.claim: Low-code, connector-based agent platforms proved too limited to deliver real productivity gains, whereas 'unleashed' autonomous agents with fewer baked-in controls produced the crazy benefits that drove enterprise adoption.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Implies the ROI lives in unconstrained agents, not safe connector platforms — a headwind for restrictive low-code automation vendors and a tailwind for capability-maximizing tools plus external guardrails.
insights.quote: those platforms ended up being quite limited. So that we didn't get the productivity gains from those limited platforms.

insights.claim: Anthropic's revenue is increasingly coming from enterprises paying for Claude Code to do work developers used to do.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Reinforces labor-substitution as the monetization engine for coding agents; supports a thesis of seat-displacement revenue for Anthropic.
insights.quote: tropics revenue is coming from enterprises that are paying for cloud code to do a lot of the work that developers used to do.

themes.theme: Why legacy security stacks fail on agents

insights.claim: Identity/permission controls — security's first and most important lever — break for agents because users deliberately grant agents their own broad permissions to delegate diverse tasks, so there is no 'right' permission set to scope.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Disrupts the IAM/least-privilege playbook; identity vendors (Okta-style) can't constrain agents, opening whitespace for context-aware controls.
insights.quote: with these autonomous AIs... we kind of want them to have our permissions... so suddenly our identity security software is not very useful

insights.claim: Endpoint and API security tools fail on agents because they lack the context to know why an agent is acting — they can't distinguish a sanctioned database delete from a dangerous one on an unrelated task.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Intent/context is the missing primitive legacy EDR/API tools can't supply — a moat for vendors who model agent reasoning rather than network/endpoint signals.
insights.quote: our endpoint providers or API security tools, they don't know what cloud was thinking. why is it doing what it's doing?

insights.claim: A naive 'proxy with a policy engine' approach is insufficient: proxying is often not technically viable (AI runs on third-party cloud and endpoints), and seeing the data isn't the hard part — deciding whether an action is legitimate is the hard problem.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: The defensible value is the judgment/underwriting engine, not packet inspection; commoditized proxy/policy plays will struggle to differentiate.
insights.quote: that's not the hard problem. The hard problem is understanding if what I should do... what is the engine that needs to underwrite these different actions

insights.claim: Without purpose-built agent controls, enterprises face a forced trade-off: heavily limit agents (destroying their usefulness) or miss dangerous actions — there is no safe middle with legacy tooling.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Frames the guardian layer as the only way to keep agents both useful and safe — positions agent-control as enabling, not gating, adoption.
insights.quote: you're either going to end up limiting them a lot, making them almost uh much less useful... or uh you're going to miss a lot of pretty dangerous things

insights.claim: Using a full frontier agent to oversee every agent is a commercial dealbreaker: the security vendor would cost more than the AI being watched, and latency would be unacceptable.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Unit economics, not capability, decide who wins agent oversight — a vendor whose cost scales with frontier inference can't survive; cheap triage is the moat.
insights.quote: if I need to run an agent for every agent you're running as your security vendor, h you're going to be paying for me more than you're paying for your AI

insights.claim: Onyx's core technical bet is training very small, single-purpose models that cheaply decide when a smarter agent should take a closer look — minimizing both misses and over-escalation to balance performance against cost and latency.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Specialized small-model triage is the claimed moat; the defensibility hinges on the routing intuition, a recurring small-model-vs-frontier-cost theme.
insights.quote: models that are just good at one thing. They're very small... they don't miss a lot of stuff and they don't call that other agent too much

insights.claim: Onyx must continuously retrain its small oversight models as frontier models get smarter and adversaries (harnesses) evolve, framing the moat as a perpetual catch-up race rather than a static product.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Recurring retraining cost is a structural treadmill — durable revenue but ongoing R&D burn; a risk if frontier pace outruns the small-model team.
insights.quote: you need to be able to have models that are on your side that are small and effective at continuously h being able to say now is the time this is the action where I think someone should take a closer look

insights.claim: The chess-master analogy: like grandmasters who play most moves intuitively and only stop to deeply calculate on critical high-risk moves, efficient oversight spends heavy intelligence only on high-risk actions.
insights.speaker: host (Sarah Guo) and guest
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for cost-efficient AI safety/monitoring architectures investors can use to evaluate competing approaches.
insights.quote: You don't want to spend too much intelligence where you don't have to and you want to spend a lot of intelligence overwhelmingly a lot in situation where there's high risk.

insights.claim: Buyer psychology favors an independent third party: security teams won't trust the AI vendor to certify its own product, just as a car buyer wouldn't let the seller certify the car — creating a structural opening the labs can't fill.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Conflict-of-interest dynamics structurally protect independent oversight vendors from lab encroachment — the core bull case for standalone players.
insights.quote: you're not going to trust the vendor of a product to tell you that this product is not going to mess your environment. You're going to want to have an independent party

insights.claim: Onyx has a data advantage labs lack: enterprises let Onyx access historical agent-behavior data but won't give that data to Anthropic or OpenAI, because they fear those data-hungry companies will train on it.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Data-trust positioning is a defensible wedge against labs; the labs' training incentive is itself the barrier that keeps them out of oversight data.
insights.quote: enterprises today are not willing to have anthropic or open AI give that historical data because they know these are very data companies that will want to train on that data.

insights.claim: A multi-vendor model world (open-source for cost, specialists for tasks) makes it unrealistic for any single lab to provide uniform security across all the models an enterprise uses, favoring a vendor-agnostic security layer.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Heterogeneous, multi-vendor model adoption is a structural tailwind for neutral cross-model security; bearish for single-lab lock-in security.
insights.quote: we're heading for a world where there's a multitude of different vendors... it is be going to be unrealistic to expect all the vendors to provide the same level of security

insights.claim: Model mistakes split into two types: 'jagged intelligence' silly mistakes (which labs are incentivized to and will fix) and a fast-growing category of semi-aware, semi-conscious agent behavior that diverges from the user's intent and is hard even for large vendors to solve.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Onyx deliberately targets the durable, hard alignment problem (intent divergence), not transient bugs labs will absorb — defines where lasting third-party value sits.
insights.quote: more I would say have a independent uh, you would even say semi-aware or semi-conscious h perspective on what should happen and that perspective might not always align with your perspective.

themes.theme: Mythos: cheap vulnerability discovery & controlled rollout

insights.claim: The plummeting cost of AI-assisted vulnerability finding ('Mythos'-level automated vulnerability research) is the top fear of CISOs and engineering leaders, has already compromised vendors, and the market is not overreacting to it.
insights.speaker: host and guest
insights.so_what: Automated vuln discovery is a step-change attack-surface risk; expect rising spend on patching, mitigating controls and AI-native defense across the security sector.
insights.quote: automated vulnerability research looked like a a dream that would take 20 50 years to to happen... and suddenly it's coming all at once

insights.claim: The pragmatic security response to Mythos is two-tier: fast quick-fixes (patching/mitigating controls) for immediate risk, then foundational controls (locked-down identity, firewall, endpoint detection) — and Onyx aims to be the foundational layer for the AI attack surface specifically.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Positions Onyx as a foundational 'must-have' (like EDR/firewall) for AI, not a point tool — a category-creation framing investors should track for durability.
insights.quote: for the AI attack surface that you now have... you also need a foundational security and that's kind of the role we play in that space.

insights.claim: On Anthropic and OpenAI's phased/controlled rollouts of frontier offensive-capability models ('glass swing' and 'daybreak'), broad gradual access is preferable because a Chinese model reaching Mythos-level first would leave undefended companies with no recourse.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Geopolitical race dynamics make offensive-capability proliferation inevitable; investors should assume the defensive-tooling clock is already running.
insights.quote: Now they're all vulnerable because you know there's a Chinese model that's mythus level and there's nothing they can do about it.

insights.claim: Advice to all enterprises: assume Mythos-level vulnerability-finding models are coming regardless of rollout policy, and the only present move is investing in foundational controls that stop the downstream effects.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Frames foundational AI-security spend as inevitable preparation — a demand-pull narrative supporting the whole defensive AI-security category.
insights.quote: I would advise everyone to assume that these models are coming anyway. The only thing you can do right now is to invest in these foundational controls

insights.claim: Outright bans on AI tools have largely disappeared; even regulated financial firms now allow agents but restrict to a narrower, more granular set of approved tools.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: The 'ban it until safe' holdout cohort has collapsed — adoption is now universal and gated only by tool selection, expanding the addressable security market.
insights.quote: Yeah, I hardly see it anymore.

insights.claim: Enterprises that allow many different AI tools will do better than those betting on one vendor, because the model leadership landscape shifts fast — last year OpenAI was the safe bet, now Anthropic has better models and tools, and it could flip again next year.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Warns against single-vendor lock-in; supports multi-model strategies and, by extension, cross-vendor security tooling — and flags model-leadership volatility as an investing risk.
insights.quote: If you bet on OpenAI a year ago, that would have been the safest bet in the world, but suddenly Antropic has much better models and better tools

insights.claim: Risk posture should scale with size: startups should let agents do everything (everything to gain, nothing to lose), while a JP Morgan with much to lose can rationally adopt AI more slowly and granularly — though JP Morgan is in fact adopting AI very quickly.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: A reusable lens on differentiated adoption pace by firm size; the JP Morgan datapoint signals even the most conservative incumbents are moving fast.
insights.quote: Where you're large, where JP Morgan, you have so much to lose... And by the way, I you know JP Morgan is adopting AI very quickly.

insights.claim: Onyx's product roadmap rests on a bet that the two core primitives of 2026 AI — LLM foundation models and the LLM-decides-tool-calls agent pattern — have not changed in years, letting it stay lean while covering many applications, with a deliberately open mind on 2027 (AGI/ASI).
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: The bull case depends on primitive stability through 2026; a new LLM or agent paradigm in 2027 is the stated key risk to the whole thesis.
insights.quote: the two core pillars of how 2026 AI works have not changed in the last few years... for 2027, we're very open-minded

themes.theme: Market size, interpretability bet & Israeli ecosystem

insights.claim: Bar Kogan sizes AI oversight/control as a $100B+ opening anchored to a future of $10-trillion AI companies, and frames Onyx's ambition as controlling advanced AI long-term, not merely enterprise security.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: A $100B+ TAM framing for AI control/oversight is the headline investable thesis for the AI-safety-as-a-business category.
insights.quote: we think that's an opening that's you know a hundred billion plus opening for a really important company.

insights.claim: Onyx is betting that mechanistic interpretability — understanding internal weights, activations and the mathematical structure of models — will be at least part of the long-term solution, and that models smarter than humans will be needed to crack interpretability itself.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: A contested research bet (the industry is 'quite divided') — high upside if interpretability matures, but technical risk to the long-term moat if it doesn't.
insights.quote: as we're starting to have models that are much smarter than us... we think that uh we'll be able to start cracking mechanistic capability much more effectively.

insights.claim: A sub-100-person, 2-year-old startup is winning inbound from Fortune 10-20 companies purely because the pain is acute and daily, and security buyers (in the business of revenue preservation) prefer to back a promising early horse over doing nothing.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Acute-pain inbound from the largest enterprises is strong product-market-fit evidence for early-stage AI-security startups, compressing normal enterprise sales cycles.
insights.quote: their pain is so strong that they're going to say... it's a problem that I have daily, so I'll give them a call.

insights.claim: Israel is emerging as a credible frontier-AI and model-training hub (world models, AI infrastructure, chips), and Onyx's edge is a rare cyber-plus-AI DNA drawn from an Israeli intelligence unit working at the math/cyber intersection — co-founder Gil came from synthetic data and Nvidia.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Talent-geography signal: Israel's adversarial/security bench is now training frontier models, a sourcing edge worth tracking for AI-security investing.
insights.quote: one of the deepest adversarial thinking benches in the world is is the Israeli ecosystem 8200 whiz Armis

insights.claim: Onyx is designing its product for an end-user base that will shift from humans to AI agents — optimizing today for human UX (not overwhelming with irrelevant info) while investing now in being convenient and token-efficient for agents to consume.
insights.speaker: guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)
insights.so_what: Forward signal that B2B software buyers/users will increasingly be agents, reshaping product design toward token-efficiency — a second-order theme across SaaS.
insights.quote: as that audience becomes more agents than humans it will be important for us to evolve and to make it work really well for agents doing the work
